metadata.title: Carbon Accounting Readiness Assessment
metadata.subtitle: Evaluate your organisation's maturity in greenhouse gas emissions measurement, reporting, and management.
metadata.description: A comprehensive 25-question assessment covering governance, organisational boundaries, emissions data quality, Scope 3 engagement, and reporting maturity. Receive a detailed maturity score, priority recommendations, and a personalised improvement roadmap.
metadata.author: Terrnix

intro.headline: Assess Your Organisation's Carbon Accounting Readiness
intro.subheadline: Most organisations know they need better carbon data but are unsure where to focus first. This assessment identifies your biggest readiness gaps and prioritises the actions that will have the greatest impact — in under 15 minutes.
benefits: Pinpoint your highest-priority carbon accounting gaps across 5 dimensions
Receive a maturity score with category-by-category breakdown
Get prioritised recommendations for your next 90 days
Download a professional PDF report to share with stakeholders
Earn a verifiable achievement certificate with unique ID
trustIndicators: Covers governance, Scope 1–3, data quality and reporting readiness
Instant executive report with PDF download
No email required to view your results

deliverables.label: Category Breakdown
deliverables.description: Per-dimension scoring across all five areas

categories.name: Governance and Accountability
categories.description: Leadership commitment, roles, responsibilities, and oversight structures for carbon accounting.

categories.name: Organisational Boundaries and Methodology
categories.description: How your organisation defines its reporting boundary and selects calculation methodologies.

categories.name: Emissions Data and Calculation Quality
categories.description: Data collection processes, quality controls, and calculation accuracy.

categories.name: Scope 3 and Supplier Engagement
categories.description: Management of value chain emissions and supplier data collection.

categories.name: Reporting, Targets and Improvement
categories.description: Disclosure practices, target-setting, and continuous improvement mechanisms.

questions.text: How clearly defined are the roles and responsibilities for carbon accounting within your organisation?
questions.helpText: Consider whether specific individuals or teams are accountable for data collection, calculation, verification, and reporting.

options.label: No assigned responsibilities; carbon accounting is ad hoc
Informal responsibilities assigned to one or two individuals without formal mandate
Documented responsibilities exist but are limited to specific departments
Clear roles defined organisation-wide with executive sponsorship
Integrated governance with board-level oversight and cross-functional accountability

questions.text: How regularly does senior leadership review carbon accounting performance and progress?
questions.helpText: Think about board reports, management meetings, or dedicated sustainability committees.

options.label: Never reviewed at senior level
Reviewed occasionally when required by external stakeholders
Reviewed annually as part of broader sustainability reporting
Reviewed quarterly with dedicated agenda items
Continuous monitoring with real-time dashboards and monthly executive briefings

questions.text: To what extent is carbon accounting integrated with financial planning and risk management?
questions.helpText: Consider whether carbon costs, carbon pricing, or climate risks are factored into business decisions.

options.label: No integration with financial or risk processes
Carbon costs tracked separately but not used in planning
Carbon considered in some investment or procurement decisions
Carbon integrated into core financial planning and risk registers
Carbon fully embedded in strategic planning, budgeting, and enterprise risk management

questions.text: How mature is your process for ensuring data quality and internal consistency in carbon accounting?
questions.helpText: Consider internal audits, data validation checks, and reconciliation procedures.

options.label: No formal quality control processes
Basic checks performed by individuals preparing the data
Documented quality control procedures applied to some data sources
Systematic quality assurance with documented sign-off and exception tracking
Automated validation, regular internal audits, and continuous improvement of data quality

questions.text: How does your organisation manage carbon accounting knowledge and capability building?
questions.helpText: Consider training programmes, documentation, and knowledge retention when staff change.

options.label: No structured knowledge management; reliant on individual expertise
Informal knowledge sharing within small teams
Documented procedures exist but training is inconsistent
Structured training programmes with regular updates and competency tracking
Comprehensive knowledge management with training, certification, and succession planning

questions.text: How consistently does your organisation document and approve its emissions calculation methodology?
questions.helpText: Consider whether you have a written methodology document that is reviewed and approved.

options.label: No documented methodology
Informal notes or spreadsheets without formal approval
Documented methodology exists but is not regularly reviewed
Approved methodology with annual review and version control
Comprehensive methodology with stakeholder input, external review, and continuous updates

questions.text: How does your organisation define its organisational boundary for emissions reporting?
questions.helpText: Consider whether you use equity share, operational control, or financial control approaches.

options.label: No defined boundary; emissions estimated for selected facilities only
Boundary defined informally without reference to established approaches
Boundary defined using equity share or operational control but not consistently applied
Clear boundary definition using established approach with documented rationale
Boundary definition aligned with GHG Protocol, regularly reviewed, and clearly communicated

questions.text: How comprehensively does your organisation track emissions across all relevant scopes?
questions.helpText: Consider Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (indirect energy), and Scope 3 (value chain) emissions.

options.label: Only Scope 1 or 2 tracked partially
Scope 1 and 2 tracked; Scope 3 not assessed
Scope 1 and 2 complete; some Scope 3 categories estimated
All three scopes assessed with most material categories quantified
Comprehensive Scope 1, 2, and 3 inventory with all material categories and regular updates

questions.text: How does your organisation select and manage emission factors for calculations?
questions.helpText: Consider whether you use national databases, supplier-specific factors, or international standards.

options.label: Emission factors selected ad hoc without documented rationale
Generic factors used without consideration of relevance or vintage
Factors selected from recognised sources but not regularly updated
Systematic factor selection with documented hierarchy and annual review
Hierarchical factor management with supplier-specific data, uncertainty analysis, and continuous improvement

questions.text: How does your organisation handle changes in boundary, methodology, or baseline year?
questions.helpText: Consider recalculation procedures, restatement policies, and documentation of changes.

options.label: Changes made without documentation or recalculation
Significant changes noted but not systematically recalculated
Recalculation performed for major changes with basic documentation
Structured change management with recalculation, documentation, and stakeholder communication
Comprehensive change protocol with automatic recalculation, audit trail, and transparent disclosure

questions.text: How systematically does your organisation collect activity data for emissions calculations?
questions.helpText: Consider fuel receipts, utility bills, travel records, and production data.

options.label: Data collected manually on an ad hoc basis
Data collected annually from key sources with manual compilation
Structured data collection with defined sources and responsibilities
Automated data feeds from major sources with validation checks
Fully integrated data collection with real-time feeds, automated validation, and exception management

questions.text: How does your organisation assess and manage uncertainty in emissions data?
questions.helpText: Consider whether you quantify uncertainty ranges, use conservative estimates, or perform sensitivity analysis.

options.label: Uncertainty not assessed or acknowledged
Uncertainty noted qualitatively without quantification
Some categories assessed for uncertainty using simple approaches
Systematic uncertainty assessment for material categories with documented ranges
Comprehensive uncertainty quantification with Monte Carlo or equivalent methods and sensitivity analysis

questions.text: How complete and accurate is your organisation's Scope 1 emissions data?
questions.helpText: Consider stationary combustion, mobile combustion, process emissions, and fugitive emissions.

options.label: Scope 1 not assessed or only partially estimated
Major sources identified but data gaps exist
All material sources tracked with some estimation for minor sources
Comprehensive Scope 1 data with documented assumptions and minimal estimation
Complete Scope 1 inventory with measured data, verified calculations, and continuous monitoring

questions.text: How does your organisation track and report Scope 2 emissions?
questions.helpText: Consider location-based and market-based methods, renewable energy certificates, and contractual instruments.

options.label: Scope 2 not tracked or only estimated
Location-based method used without market-based assessment
Both location-based and market-based methods calculated but not reconciled
Both methods reported with clear documentation of contractual instruments
Dual reporting with verified instruments, quality criteria, and strategic procurement alignment

questions.text: How does your organisation verify the accuracy of its emissions calculations?
questions.helpText: Consider internal audits, third-party verification, or assurance statements.

options.label: No verification performed
Informal review by colleagues without structured process
Internal audit or review with documented findings
Independent third-party verification or limited assurance
Annual external assurance with detailed findings and continuous improvement tracking

questions.text: How comprehensively has your organisation screened its Scope 3 emissions?
questions.helpText: Consider whether you have assessed all 15 GHG Protocol Scope 3 categories.

options.label: Scope 3 not assessed
High-level screening of a few categories only
Most categories screened with identification of material sources
All 15 categories assessed with quantification of material categories
Comprehensive Scope 3 assessment with all categories quantified, prioritised, and regularly updated

questions.text: How does your organisation engage suppliers to collect emissions data?
questions.helpText: Consider supplier surveys, contractual requirements, or collaborative programmes.

options.label: No supplier engagement on emissions
Ad hoc requests to key suppliers without systematic follow-up
Structured supplier surveys for major suppliers with defined timelines
Systematic supplier engagement programme with data quality requirements and support
Integrated supplier platform with automated data collection, validation, and performance tracking

questions.text: How does your organisation use spend-based versus activity-based methods for Scope 3?
questions.helpText: Consider whether you use EEIO models, supplier-specific data, or hybrid approaches.

options.label: Only spend-based methods used without validation
Spend-based methods with some activity data for key categories
Hybrid approach with activity data for material categories and spend-based for others
Activity-based preferred with documented hierarchy and improvement plans
Activity-based as standard with supplier-specific data, continuous improvement, and uncertainty quantification

questions.text: How does your organisation manage data quality for Scope 3 emissions?
questions.helpText: Consider data collection protocols, quality ratings, and gap-filling procedures.

options.label: No data quality management for Scope 3
Basic quality checks applied inconsistently
Documented quality criteria applied to major categories
Systematic quality management with ratings, gap-filling protocols, and improvement tracking
Comprehensive quality framework with automated validation, supplier scoring, and continuous data improvement

questions.text: How does your organisation set and track Scope 3 reduction targets?
questions.helpText: Consider whether targets are science-based, time-bound, and linked to supplier engagement.

options.label: No Scope 3 targets set
Informal targets without formal commitment or timeline
Formal targets set but not yet integrated with supplier programmes
Science-based targets with supplier engagement and regular progress reporting
Ambitious science-based targets with supplier mandates, incentives, and transparent progress disclosure

questions.text: How does your organisation disclose its greenhouse gas emissions?
questions.helpText: Consider annual reports, sustainability reports, CDP submissions, or regulatory filings.

options.label: No formal disclosure of emissions data
Limited disclosure in internal documents only
Disclosed in sustainability report or website with basic metrics
Comprehensive disclosure aligned with recognised frameworks (GHG Protocol, GRI, CDP)
Full disclosure with third-party assurance, ESRS alignment, and transparent methodology

questions.text: How does your organisation set and manage emissions reduction targets?
questions.helpText: Consider whether targets are science-based, time-bound, and cover relevant scopes.

options.label: No emissions reduction targets
Informal or aspirational targets without baseline or timeline
Formal targets with baseline and timeline but not science-based
Science-based targets covering relevant scopes with regular progress tracking
Ambitious science-based targets with net-zero commitment, interim milestones, and transparent reporting

questions.text: How does your organisation track progress against emissions targets?
questions.helpText: Consider KPI dashboards, regular reporting cycles, and variance analysis.

options.label: Progress not tracked systematically
Progress reviewed annually with basic variance analysis
Quarterly progress tracking with identified gaps and corrective actions
Monthly tracking with dashboards, trend analysis, and management reporting
Real-time tracking with predictive analytics, scenario modelling, and automated alerts

questions.text: How does your organisation integrate carbon performance into broader sustainability or ESG reporting?
questions.helpText: Consider whether carbon data is reported alongside other ESG metrics.

options.label: Carbon reported separately without ESG integration
Carbon included in sustainability report but not linked to other metrics
Carbon integrated with some ESG metrics in a unified report
Comprehensive ESG reporting with carbon as a core metric and clear linkages
Fully integrated ESG reporting with carbon linked to strategy, risk, and financial performance

questions.text: How does your organisation drive continuous improvement in carbon accounting and reporting?
questions.helpText: Consider feedback mechanisms, benchmarking, technology upgrades, and process refinement.

options.label: No systematic improvement process
Improvements made reactively when issues are identified
Annual review of processes with planned improvements
Structured improvement programme with benchmarking, technology roadmap, and regular updates
Continuous improvement culture with innovation, automation, and leading practice adoption

maturityLevels.label: Initial
maturityLevels.description: Carbon accounting is informal, ad hoc, or not yet established. Significant foundational work is needed.
maturityLevels.executiveSummary: Your organisation is at the beginning of its carbon accounting journey. Processes are informal or non-existent, and emissions data is likely incomplete or unreliable. Focus on establishing basic governance, defining boundaries, and implementing systematic data collection.
typicalStrengths: Awareness of carbon accounting as a business priority
Some individual expertise or interest exists
commonRisks: Regulatory non-compliance in jurisdictions with mandatory reporting
Inability to respond to customer or investor data requests
Missed cost-saving opportunities from efficiency improvements
immediateActions: Assign clear ownership for carbon accounting to a specific individual or team
Document your organisational boundary and reporting scope
Implement a basic data collection process for Scope 1 and 2 emissions
roadmap30: Establish governance structure with executive sponsor
Complete Scope 1 and 2 inventory using recognised emission factors
Document calculation methodology and data sources
roadmap90: Screen Scope 3 categories to identify material sources
Set initial emissions baseline and reduction target
Begin supplier engagement for priority Scope 3 categories

maturityLevels.label: Developing
maturityLevels.description: Basic carbon accounting processes exist but are inconsistent or incomplete. Improvement is underway but not yet systematic.
maturityLevels.executiveSummary: Your organisation has established basic carbon accounting processes but they remain inconsistent or limited in scope. You likely have a partial emissions inventory and some governance structures. The priority now is to standardise processes, expand data coverage, and strengthen quality controls.
typicalStrengths: Basic emissions data collected for key sources
commonRisks: Data gaps may lead to material underestimation of emissions
Inconsistent methodology makes year-on-year comparison difficult
Limited Scope 3 coverage may understate total footprint
immediateActions: Standardise data collection procedures across all relevant departments
Complete Scope 1 and 2 inventory with documented assumptions
Screen all 15 Scope 3 categories to identify material sources
roadmap30: Implement quality control procedures for data collection and calculation
Quantify material Scope 3 categories using available data
Document and approve formal calculation methodology
roadmap90: Set science-based reduction targets with clear timelines
Begin structured supplier engagement for priority categories
Prepare for external verification or assurance

maturityLevels.label: Established
maturityLevels.description: Carbon accounting processes are documented and applied consistently. Most material emissions are quantified and reported.
maturityLevels.executiveSummary: Your organisation has established carbon accounting as a core business process. You have documented methodologies, consistent data collection, and regular reporting. The focus now should be on expanding Scope 3 coverage, improving data quality, and aligning with advanced frameworks.
typicalStrengths: Documented methodology with defined boundaries and emission factors
Regular reporting with reasonable data completeness
commonRisks: Scope 3 data quality may limit accuracy of total footprint
Supplier engagement may be insufficient for ambitious targets
Integration with financial planning may be limited
immediateActions: Improve Scope 3 data quality through supplier-specific data collection
Align reporting with ESRS or other recognised frameworks
Integrate carbon performance into management dashboards
roadmap30: Obtain external verification or limited assurance
Set science-based targets with clear reduction pathways
roadmap90: Achieve comprehensive Scope 3 quantification for material categories
Integrate carbon accounting with enterprise risk management
Explore advanced data collection technologies and automation

maturityLevels.label: Advanced
maturityLevels.description: Carbon accounting is mature, comprehensive, and well-integrated. External verification is likely in place.
maturityLevels.executiveSummary: Your organisation demonstrates advanced carbon accounting maturity. You have comprehensive inventories, strong governance, external verification, and integration with strategic planning. The priority is to maintain excellence, drive supplier transformation, and prepare for emerging regulatory requirements.
typicalStrengths: Comprehensive Scope 1, 2, and 3 inventory with good data quality
Science-based targets with clear progress tracking
commonRisks: Maintaining data quality as operations expand or change
Keeping pace with evolving regulatory requirements
Ensuring supplier data keeps up with target ambitions
immediateActions: Enhance supplier data collection through platform integration
Align with emerging ESRS and CSRD requirements
Develop scenario analysis and climate risk assessment capabilities
roadmap30: Implement automated data collection and validation systems
Expand supplier engagement to secondary and tertiary tiers
Develop internal carbon pricing for investment decisions
roadmap90: Lead industry collaboration on Scope 3 data standards
Integrate climate scenario analysis into strategic planning

maturityLevels.label: Leading
maturityLevels.description: Carbon accounting is a strategic capability with continuous innovation, full integration, and industry leadership.
maturityLevels.executiveSummary: Your organisation is a leader in carbon accounting. You have comprehensive, verified inventories, ambitious science-based targets, deep supplier engagement, and full integration with business strategy. Focus on maintaining leadership, innovating in data and methodology, and supporting industry transformation.
typicalStrengths: Comprehensive, verified emissions inventory across all scopes
Ambitious science-based targets with clear pathways
Deep supplier engagement and value chain transformation
commonRisks: Complacency or failure to adapt to new regulatory requirements
Difficulty maintaining data quality at scale
Balancing ambition with practical implementation challenges
immediateActions: Share best practices and support industry-wide data standards
Invest in next-generation data collection and AI-powered analytics
Develop thought leadership on carbon accounting innovation
roadmap30: Pilot advanced technologies for real-time emissions monitoring
Expand collaboration with peers and industry bodies
Develop proprietary tools or methodologies for competitive advantage
roadmap90: Achieve recognised industry leadership in carbon accounting
Contribute to standard-setting bodies and regulatory consultations
Build sustainable competitive advantage through carbon excellence
